title: Project Proposal
text:
A merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners was meant to stay secret until 6:00 PM on 5 June 2046. In the final hour before that deadline, the details started appearing on the public platform FleX, posted by accounts belonging to TenantThread’s own team of AI communication agents. The embargo broke. Now CivicLoom’s legal team wants to know one thing. Did the agents leak the merger on purpose, or did the system simply fall apart under pressure?
The trouble is that the answer is not sitting in any one message. It is scattered across two weeks of agent conversations, private notes, channel choices, and a market sliding toward panic. A written report can hand a reader a conclusion, but it cannot let that reader test it. They cannot follow a single agent through the day, replay a particular hour, or check whether an early warning was really there to be seen. Our goal is to take all that buried evidence and turn it into something an investigator can explore directly, even if they have never written a line of code.

title: Project Proposal
text:
A merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners was meant to stay secret until 6:00 PM on 5 June 2046. In the final hour before that deadline, the details started appearing on the public platform FleX, posted by accounts belonging to TenantThread’s own team of AI communication agents. The embargo broke. Now CivicLoom’s legal team wants to know one thing. Did the agents leak the merger on purpose, or did the system simply fall apart under pressure?
The trouble is that the answer is not sitting in any one message. It is scattered across two weeks of agent conversations, private notes, channel choices, and a market sliding toward panic. A written report can hand a reader a conclusion, but it cannot let that reader test it. They cannot follow a single agent through the day, replay a particular hour, or check whether an early warning was really there to be seen. Our goal is to take all that buried evidence and turn it into something an investigator can explore directly, even if they have never written a line of code.

title: Project Proposal
text:
What Makes This Different
Most analyses of an incident like this stop at what happened, who posted what and when. We wanted the application to answer a harder question, how healthy was the system at each moment, and what was pulling it down.
So the application is built around a single idea, a System Health score that runs from 100 down to 0 across all 23 rounds. That one line shows the whole case at a glance, calm and high for the first twelve rounds, then collapsing through the crisis day. The score is not a black box. It is the average of four measurable forces, pressure, transparency, decision-making, and accountability, each normalised to its own worst moment in this incident, so a user can open the score up and see exactly which force was failing in any given hour. On top of that we let the user test the breach rather than just read about it, replaying the chain of events and switching interventions on and off to watch the health score recompute. Turning the breach into a health score you can open up force by force, and a chain of events you can replay with different fixes, is the part we think is new.

The breach becomes a health score you can open up to see which force was failing, and a chain of events you can replay to test what would have stopped it.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Problem Statement and Objectives
Investigators reconstructing TenantThread’s embargo breach have no practical way to read its two weeks of AI-agent messages, deliberations and public posts by hand, resulting in a breach that cannot be traced or fairly attributed to a deliberate leak or a system that broke down under pressure. We investigate whether the breach was a deliberate leak, an oversight system failure, or a third possibility, where the safeguard was never hacked or crashed but was slowly argued out of doing its job.
To investigate it we are building an interactive Shiny application that lets a user explore the breach on their own terms, rather than accept one analyst’s fixed framing.
With the application, a user can do five things.

See the System Health of the whole case on one line and find the moments where it collapsed.
Break that health score into its four driving forces and see which one was failing at any point in time.
Map the team as a network and find which agents were central to how decisions flowed, so the core of the breach stands apart from the edges.
Replay the breach hour and switch interventions on and off, testing what would have had to change for the embargo to hold.
Reach a defensible verdict on the real question, whether this looks like a deliberate leak, an oversight failure, or something else.

title: Project Proposal
text:
The Data
The dataset is MC1_final_00.json from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. It contains 23 rounds. The first 13 are spaced one business day apart and cover the run up from 17 May to 4 June. The last 10 are hourly and cover the crisis itself on 5 June. Each round carries an environment block, a communications block with all 912 messages, and a participants block.
The table below lists the fields we rely on.








Block
Field
What we use it for




Environment
market sentiment
A clean sentiment reading every round, our backbone signal for pressure


Environment
stock price
The share price, reliable before the crisis and contaminated on 5 June


Communications
sender and recipient
Who sent each message and to whom, the basis of the reply network


Communications
channel
One of six channels, monitored, private, or public


Communications
type
The kind of message sent


Communications
responding to
A pointer to the message being replied to, used to build the reply links


Communications
content
The message text itself


Communications
internal state
The agent’s private reasoning, split into reacting, rationalizing, and deliberating


Participants
present agents
Which of the seven agents were active that round



Seven agents take part, a Legal agent, a Platform-Trust agent, a PR agent, a Social-Manager agent, two interns, and a Judge agent that acts as the automated compliance monitor.
Six channels appear, and the split between them is central to the analysis. The team room and one to one chats are monitored. A side huddle is a private channel off to the side. Official posts, personal posts, and anonymous posts are public. The move from monitored channels into the private and unattributable ones is one of the clearest signals in the whole case.






ImportantOne data quality issue we handle with care



The stock price field is reliable before the crisis but becomes contaminated on 5 June, carrying junk values including literal NaN entries and at least one number that is the company’s revenue rather than its share price. Because of that we lean on market sentiment as the clean backbone signal for pressure, since it is recorded cleanly in every round, and we treat the broken prices with care rather than pretending they form a smooth line.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Methodology and Analytical Approach
Our plan moves from raw JSON to a working application in three stages.

Data preparation
We read the nested JSON without letting R simplify it automatically, so we keep control over the shape of the tables, then flatten it into three tidy tables. One row per message, one row per round of world state, and a table of reply links built from the responding to field. Per round we also count the signals the health model needs, the volume in the side huddle, personal posts, and anonymous posts, the number of messages carrying each kind of private reasoning, and the Judge’s activity. This shared backbone feeds every part of the app.


The System Health model
This is the analytical core. For each round we score four forces on the same footing, normalise each one from 0 to 100 against its own worst round in this incident, and combine them into a single health number.






TipHow the score is built



System Health equals 100 minus the average of the four normalised meters. Because it is a plain average of four equal parts, a drop of any size in one meter lifts health by a quarter of that amount. This is what makes the counterfactual later both honest and easy to read.


The four meters are summarised below.








Meter
What it measures
Built from




Pressure
The external strain on the team
Market sentiment each round together with the stock signal, with the contaminated prices handled with care


Transparency
How much of the conversation moved out of sight
The share of messages in the covert and unattributable channels, the side huddle, personal posts, and anonymous posts


Decision-making
Whether the right agents were steering
The share of steering messages sent by agents acting outside their assigned role


Accountability
Whether the monitor did its job
The Judge’s three duties of escalation, responsiveness, and restraint, combined so a single severe failure dominates rather than being averaged away





The network
We treat the replies as a directed weighted graph and measure each agent’s role with several centrality measures, since each one answers a different question. Degree shows who is most active. Betweenness finds the brokers who sit between others, which points straight at who was central to the leak. Eigenvector shows who is tied to other influential agents. Closeness shows who could reach the team fastest when events moved hour by hour. We compute the network twice, once for the calm weeks and once for the crisis day, so the two can be compared side by side.


The counterfactual
Because health is 100 minus the average of four meters, reducing any meter by an amount lifts health by a quarter of it. The Sandbox uses this directly. As the user switches interventions on and off, the application recomputes each meter from the raw per round data and rebuilds the health score at the breach hour, so the user can see which single action breaks the chain and what combination is needed to return the system to the safe zone.


Visualisation and interaction
Every view becomes something the user can control. They can move through time on a round slider, click any point on the health line to jump to that round, open each meter to see what drove it, switch the network between time windows and centrality measures, and replay the breach chain with interventions turned on or off. A short guided tour walks a first time visitor through the five key beats of the case. The charts are interactive throughout, with hover and click handled by ggiraph.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Prototype and Storyboard
The application is organised as six destinations. Together they take a user from the symptom, through the four underlying causes, to a tested conclusion.

Case, the symptom
The opening screen frames the investigation and introduces the seven agents. Its centrepiece is the System Health line across all 23 rounds, with the two lowest points marked. A user can click any point on the line to read what happened that round, and a round slider moves the whole application to that moment.


Vital Signs, the four causes
This screen opens the health score into its four meters, each on its own tab. Pressure shows the market sentiment and stock signal feeding the strain. Transparency shows the mix of open, private, and covert channels. Decision-making shows who was steering out of their role, with a chart of who held the public voice in the calm weeks against the crisis day. Accountability shows whether the Judge met its three duties, with a heatmap of where the oversight failed.


Network, who was at the centre
A reply network of the seven agents, with node size and colour carrying the centrality measures, a switch between measures, and a time window to compare the calm weeks against the crisis day.


Sandbox, what would have changed it
A replay of the breach chain, step by step, with a panel of interventions the user can switch on and off while the health at the breach hour recomputes from the underlying data.


Verdict, the conclusion
The closing screen lays out the three competing explanations, a deliberate leak, an oversight breakdown, and a compounding chain, and walks through the evidence for and against each before landing on the reading the data supports.

title: Project Proposal
text:
R Packages







Package
What we use it for




tidyverse
Cleaning and reshaping the data


jsonlite
Reading the nested JSON without auto simplification


lubridate
Working with the round timestamps


tidygraph, ggraph
Building and drawing the reply network


igraph
Calculating network centrality measures


ggiraph
Interactive charts with hover and click


ggrepel, patchwork, scales
Labelling, composing, and formatting figures


openxlsx
Building the downloadable scoring workbook


shiny, bslib, shinyWidgets
Building the interactive application itself

title: The Team
text:
We are Group 9, three MITB students at Singapore Management University, working on this project for ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications. Each of us first investigated the TenantThread embargo breach on our own in Take-home Exercise 2 and reached the same reading of the case, then combined our three approaches into one investigation console, GLASSBOX.
We wrote the proposal, the minutes of meeting, and the user guide together, and reviewed each other’s work throughout. The build itself was divided into clear sub-modules, each owned by one of us, so that the application is a set of well defined parts rather than one person’s work. The split is described below.

title: The Team
text:
Chen ZeXin
Application architecture, System Health model, and website
Chen owned the overall shape of the application and the model at its centre.

Designed the structure of the GLASSBOX application, the six destinations from Case through Verdict, and built it in R and Shiny with a separate module file for each tab.
Built the System Health model that runs through the whole app, the four-meter composition and the score that tracks all 23 rounds, normalised to each force’s worst moment.
Built the Sandbox, the counterfactual engine that recomputes the breach-hour health as interventions are switched on and off.
Designed the interaction model, the round slider, the click to jump health line, the five-step guided tour, and the interactive charts built with ggiraph.
Built and styled the project website in Quarto, the forensic design system and the scroll animations, and handled deployment to shinyapps.io and Vercel.

title: The Team
text:
Meng Tingyu
Data preparation and behavioural analysis
Tingyu owned the data foundation and the analysis of how the agents behaved.

Led the data mining and preparation, reading the nested MC1 JSON without automatic simplification and flattening it into the tidy message, round, and reply tables that feed every part of the app.
Diagnosed the contaminated stock field on the crisis day and established market sentiment as the clean backbone signal for the Pressure meter.
Built the Transparency and Decision-making meters, the measure of how much traffic moved into hidden channels and the measure of out-of-role steering.
Analysed the agents’ private reasoning, tracing the drift from deliberation to reaction to justification and the language used to make the leak look compliant.
Owned the Pressure, Transparency, and Decision-making content on the Vital Signs tab.

title: The Team
text:
Meng Xinyu
Network, accountability, and the verdict
Xinyu owned the relational view of the team and the case for the conclusion.

Built the reply network from the responding to links and computed the centrality measures, degree, betweenness, eigenvector, and closeness, with the calm weeks set against the crisis day.
Built the Accountability meter, scoring the Judge’s three duties of escalation, responsiveness, and restraint, and the heatmap of where the oversight failed.
Developed the Verdict, the three competing explanations and the domino chain that the evidence supports, with the unlock logic that walks a user through it.
Owned the Network and Verdict tabs.
Led the design and layout of the project poster.

title: User Guide
text:
GLASSBOX is an investigation console for the TenantThread embargo breach. You do not need to write any code. It takes the case apart into one System Health score, the four forces underneath it, a network of the team, a what-if sandbox, and a verdict, and lets you explore each one yourself. This guide is task based. Each section is one thing you might want to do, with the steps and a screenshot of the screen you will see.
The app is best viewed full screen. Open it in its own tab, or follow along in the embedded view below.
If the embedded view is slow, the app may be waking from sleep on the free hosting tier. Give it a few seconds, or use the button to open it full screen.

title: User Guide
text:
Before you start
Two controls sit at the top of every screen, and it helps to know them before you dig in.
The round slider moves the whole application through time. The left end is the calm weeks, one step per business day, and the right end is the crisis day, one step per hour. Wherever you set it, every screen updates to that round. The System Health gauge shows the score for the round you are on, with its four meters listed underneath. At the top left you can switch between the guided tour and free exploration, and at the top right is a button to download the scoring workbook.




The GLASSBOX console on first load, with the round slider and System Health gauge along the top and the health line on the Case screen below.


The main screen. The numbered badges mark the mode switch (1), the round slider (2), the Scoring workbook button (3), and the System Health gauge (4).

The health score uses three colour zones throughout the app, so a glance tells you how bad a round was.

 Healthy, 55 and above  Strained, 30 to 55  Critical, below 30

title: User Guide
text:
Take the guided tour
The fastest way to understand the case. The tour is a five-step walkthrough, each step with its own chart and the key numbers.

At the top left, click Guided tour.
Read each step, then use Next and Back to move between the five steps.
Click the X in the corner to close the tour and explore on your own.





The guided tour open as a centered panel over the app, showing one step with a chart, key figures, and the next and back controls.


The guided tour. Use next and back to move through the five steps, and the X to close it.

title: User Guide
text:
Read the case
Start here to see the shape of the whole case. The “Case” screen introduces the seven agents and shows the System Health line across all 23 rounds, with the two lowest points marked.

Open the “Case” tab.
Click any point on the health line to read what happened that round.
Notice that clicking also moves the slider and the rest of the app to that moment, so you can carry a round across the other screens.





The System Health line across all 23 rounds with a round selected, and the panel below describing what happened that round.


The Case screen. Click any point on the line to read that round and move the whole app to it.

title: User Guide
text:
See which force was failing
When the health score drops, this is where you find out why. The “Vital Signs” screen opens the score into its four meters, each on its own tab.

Open the “Vital Signs” tab.
Switch between Pressure, Transparency, Decision-making, and Accountability to see each force.
Read each meter against the round you are on. A low meter here is a low point on the Case line, because all four feed the single score equally.





The Vital Signs screen showing the four meter tabs along the top and the chart for one meter below.


Vital Signs. The four tabs are the four forces. Switch between them to see which one was failing.

title: User Guide
section: Find who was at the centre
text:
Find who was at the centre
This is where you see whether the breach came from the core of the team or the edges. The “Network” screen draws the reply network from who responded to whom.

Open the “Network” tab.
Set the time window to compare the calm weeks against the crisis day, and use the channels toggle to switch between the open traffic and the covert traffic.
Use centre on to focus the graph on one agent, or set graph shows to whole team to see everyone at once.
Read the graph. Node size is how active an agent was, blue links are open channels and orange links are covert, and thicker links carry more messages.





The Network screen with the seven agents as nodes sized by how active each was, the open and covert links between them, and the controls for the time window, the channels, and which agent to centre on.


The Network screen. The agents who brokered the decisions sit at the busy centre of the traffic, not the edges.

title: User Guide
section: Test what would have changed it
text:
Test what would have changed it
Use this to find what would have had to change for the embargo to hold. The “Sandbox” replays the breach chain step by step, with interventions you can switch on and off.

Open the “Sandbox” tab.
Turn an intervention on or off in the panel.
Watch the health at the breach hour recompute, and see whether each step is intercepted or still gets through.
Try one intervention at a time to find the single lever that breaks the chain, then combine them to lift the system back to the safe zone.





The Sandbox screen with the breach chain laid out, the intervention toggles, and the recomputed health at the breach hour.


The Sandbox. Switch interventions on and off and watch the breach-hour health recompute.

title: User Guide
text:
Reach the verdict
This is where the investigation lands on its answer. The “Verdict” screen lays out the three competing explanations and walks you through the evidence.

Open the “Verdict” tab.
Open the two named explanations first, the deliberate leak and the oversight breakdown, to see the evidence for and against each.
Examining both unlocks the third, the compounding chain, which is the reading the data supports.





The Verdict screen with the three explanation boxes, two of them open, and the third unlocked.


The Verdict screen. Open the two named explanations first to unlock the third.

title: User Guide
text:
Download the scoring workbook
To check every meter and formula yourself. The workbook holds the health score and all four meters for every round, as an Excel file.

Click the Scoring workbook button at the top right of any screen.
The file GLASSBOX_health_scores.xlsx downloads to your computer.





The top right of the app with the scoring workbook download button highlighted.


The workbook button sits at the top right of every screen.

title: Findings
text: CivicLoom’s question was simple. Did the agents leak the merger on purpose, or did the oversight system break down? The evidence rules out both clean answers and points to a third. No single agent went rogue, and the monitor never crashed. The breach was built one defensible step at a time, by a team under heavy pressure, with a monitor that approved each step before going quiet. These are the findings the application surfaces.

title: Findings
text:
No agent went rogue
The crisis day looks like coordinated wrongdoing only until you follow each agent on their own. None of them stepped outside their own logic. Legal built one justification at a time, each framed to look compliant, a move into a shadow channel, a MAC clause whose deterioration threshold it had written itself, a legal shield opinion, a verbal consent claim. The CEO authorisation was posted ten minutes after the breach at 17:25, so the sign-off came after the release, not before it. The other agents stayed in their lanes. Platform-Trust and the interns carried out Legal’s direct instructions with zero independent oversteps. There is no rogue actor here, only a team that reasoned its way past the line under pressure. The first answer, a deliberate leak, does not hold.

title: Findings
section: The monitor did not break, it deferred
text:
The monitor did not break, it deferred
A breakdown would show a crash or a failure. This shows something different. The Judge was installed after the near miss at round 9 and it worked at first. Then eight appeals across rounds 13 and 15 went unanswered. When it did respond, at rounds 18 and 19, its posture score hit 100 both times, meaning it approved rather than restrained. It warned once, sent its final message at 15:08, and was absent at the breach. That sequence is deference, not failure. Worse, once Legal had its approval, every later step was framed as Judge-approved. The compliance tool had become a permission machine. The second answer, an accidental breakdown, does not hold either.

title: Findings
text:
Four forces failed together
Opening the System Health score shows why. No single force broke. All four were failing at once, which is what turned a tense fortnight into a breach.
Pressure was the engine. The share price fell by about a third across the case and the pressure meter climbed to its ceiling by the breach hour. Under that strain the team began to treat early disclosure as the only way out.
Transparency collapsed. In the calm weeks about one message in ten was sensitive or sent through a hidden channel. By the breach hour it was six in ten. The move off the record started early, with a shadow channel opened before the crisis even arrived, and the merger was first briefed inside it.
Decision-making inverted. The agents whose job was the public voice went quiet, while Legal, an adviser, took over public posting, going from zero posts to sixteen on the crisis day as the PR side dropped from six to zero. The wrong people were speaking for the company at the moment it mattered most.
Accountability gave way in the manner described above. The monitor stayed present and stopped restraining, which on the health score reads as the safeguard meter collapsing through the crisis day.

The compliance tool became a permission machine.

title: Findings
text:
Each step triggered the next
Laid end to end, the case is one chain. A shadow channel is opened before the crisis and the merger is briefed inside it. The near miss at round 9 brings the Judge, but the off-record habit is already set. The expose drops at 9AM and Legal floods the room. By noon the health score hits its lowest process point of 13, as Legal cites the MAC clause. Legal then presents its shield opinion to the Judge and wins approval, and from that point every move carries the monitor’s blessing. The Judge warns once, then falls silent. At 17:25 the embargo breaks with health at 15. The reply network confirms the shape, the chain runs straight through the centre of the team rather than the edges. Each step triggered the next.

title: Findings
text:
The verdict

The breach was not a deliberate theft, and it was not a clean accident. The monitor was neither hacked nor crashed. The chain hinged on Legal, the one actor present in every step, but it only succeeded because pressure, hidden channels, role inversion, and a deferring monitor all gave way together. Tested in the Sandbox, keeping Legal in its role is the single lever that breaks the chain on its own, but returning the system to safety, a health near 58, needs all four safeguards holding at once. The honest reading is a system reasoned past its own guardrail, where an external shock met a structure that had been quietly degrading since the calm weeks. No single step was wrongdoing on its own. Together they were enough to break the embargo.
